refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation (communication integrity) as the core functional requirement, removing the implicit 'support teleoperation' which is a higher-level system capability. The phrase 'communication quality meeting the customer-defined packet loss limit' is vague; 'maintain communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits' is more precise and solution-neutral. The addition of 'during agreed network conditions' acknowledges the acceptance criterion context without prescribing how it is achieved.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions across agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) and measure end-to-end packet loss rate. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions and packet loss metrics in test report.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit teleoperation control commands from the tele-operator interface to the remote equipment with fidelity sufficient to preserve operator intent.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit telemetry and sensor data from the remote equipment to the tele-operator interface with fidelity sufficient to support situational awareness.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication loss or degradation events to the operator and safety system.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and customer-configurable thresholds
DP2: Employ forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels tuned to measured network conditions
DP3: Establish dual independent communication paths with automatic failover and load balancing to reduce single-path loss impact
DP4: Deploy jitter buffering and packet reordering logic with timeout-based loss detection and on-demand retransmission
DP5: Implement network condition monitoring and dynamic codec/payload adaptation to reduce packet size and loss sensitivity
DP6: Use a hybrid approach combining selective retransmission (for critical control frames) and FEC (for telemetry streams) with customer-tunable parameters
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP6: Implement a hybrid loss mitigation strategy that combines selective retransmission for critical control frames (operator commands, safety signals) with configurable forward error correction for telemetry and status streams, supported by continuous network condition monitoring and customer-defined loss thresholds that trigger adaptive parameter adjustment.

potential_sources: Coupling between loss detection logic and retransmission scheduling—mitigated by separating detection (passive monitoring) from action (adaptive retry policy)
Coupling between FEC encoder/decoder and payload serialization—mitigated by treating FEC as a transparent layer above serialization
Coupling between network monitoring and parameter tuning—mitigated by defining monitoring as a separate concern that feeds into a configuration service
Coupling between critical vs. non-critical frame classification and application logic—mitigated by defining frame priority at the communication protocol layer, not in application code
coupling_check.notes: DP6 achieves low coupling by separating concerns: (1) detection (network monitoring), (2) classification (frame priority), (3) mitigation (FEC + retransmission), and (4) tuning (customer-configurable thresholds). Each concern can be designed and tested independently. The hybrid approach avoids over-reliance on a single mechanism, reducing brittleness.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss target and network conditions are measurable and stable during operation; RISK: If conditions are highly variable or unmeasurable, adaptive tuning may oscillate or fail to converge.
ASSUMPTION: Critical control frames can be reliably identified and prioritized; RISK: Misclassification of frame priority could lead to safety-critical loss.

ASSUMPTION: Retransmission latency budget exists (i.e., operator can tolerate bounded delay for retry); RISK: If latency is extremely tight, retransmission may not be viable; FEC alone may be required.
ASSUMPTION: Dual paths or sufficient bandwidth headroom exists for redundancy; RISK: If network is severely constrained, adding FEC or retransmission overhead may worsen congestion.
ASSUMPTION: Customer has capability to define and validate loss thresholds; RISK: Poorly chosen thresholds may lead to over-provisioning or under-protection.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits graceful degradation or fallback modes if loss exceeds threshold; RISK: If zero-loss is mandated, DP6 may not satisfy acceptance criteria under all conditions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) acceptable latency budget for retransmission, (2) definition of 'critical' vs. 'non-critical' frames in the context of safety and operational requirements, (3) expected network condition ranges (latency, jitter, bandwidth), and (4) fallback behavior if packet loss exceeds the target despite mitigation. Additionally, clarify whether the 1% loss target applies to all frames or only critical control frames. This will enable tighter DP specification and reduce ambiguity in IM design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates network resilience logic including selective retransmission for critical control frames, configurable forward error correction for telemetry/status streams, network condition monitoring, and adaptive parameter adjustment based on customer-defined loss thresholds.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and control frames; Teleoperation module applies selective retransmission strategy to ensure critical commands reach Vehicle with high reliability.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety signals requiring critical-frame treatment; Teleoperation module prioritizes safety signal retransmission and reports network-induced safety degradation back to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle telemetry and status streams with configurable FEC applied; Teleoperation module adapts FEC parameters based on monitored network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides real-time network condition metrics (loss rate, latency, jitter); Teleoperation module monitors this interface continuously and adjusts loss mitigation parameters dynamically.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Teleoperation is the natural owner of network resilience for remote control and telemetry. Selective retransmission of operator commands and safety signals aligns with Teleoperation's responsibility for command delivery. FEC for telemetry streams is a core concern. Network monitoring and adaptive adjustment are localized within this module. Minor diffusion: Safety Services must be notified of network-induced safety degradation (SafetyAlert interface), but this is a clean outbound notification, not deep coupling.

Moderate fit but poor ownership. Safety Services owns safety logic and thresholds, but network resilience mechanisms (retransmission, FEC, monitoring) are not safety-specific concerns—they are network transport concerns. Placing hybrid loss mitigation here would diffuse network engineering logic into a safety module, violating separation of concerns. Safety Services should consume the outputs (e.g., network health status) but not own the mechanism.

Poor fit. Vehicle owns vehicle-side logic and actuators. Network resilience is a communication-layer concern, not a vehicle-side concern. Placing loss mitigation here would require Vehicle to manage operator command retransmission and telemetry FEC, which are upstream of the vehicle. High diffusion: Vehicle would need to understand network conditions and customer loss thresholds, creating tight coupling to network state.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level container. While it could theoretically host network resilience, it is too broad and would not provide clear ownership or interface boundaries. Placing DP6 here would obscure the module responsible for teleoperation-specific network handling and make it harder to test, maintain, and evolve the loss mitigation strategy independently.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of hybrid loss mitigation because it is responsible for reliable delivery of operator commands (critical control frames) and collection of vehicle telemetry (status streams). Network condition monitoring and adaptive parameter adjustment are localized concerns within this module. The module interfaces cleanly with Tasking (inbound commands), SafetyAlert (inbound safety signals and outbound degradation notifications), Telemetry (outbound streams with FEC), and Network Link (continuous monitoring). Selective retransmission of critical frames and configurable FEC are both teleoperation-layer concerns, not vehicle-side or safety-logic concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: Safety Services may need to override or adjust loss thresholds in response to safety-critical scenarios. Mitigation: Define a SafetyAlert interface that allows Safety Services to signal heightened criticality, triggering Teleoperation to increase retransmission aggressiveness for safety frames.

Risk: Customer-defined loss thresholds may need to be managed at a higher level (NGHE Solution) for multi-vehicle or fleet-wide policies. Mitigation: Teleoperation accepts threshold parameters via configuration interface; NGHE Solution or a configuration service provides these parameters at initialization or via dynamic update.

Risk: Network Link interface may not expose sufficient granularity of network metrics (e.g., per-stream loss rates vs. aggregate loss). Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface is designed to provide stream-specific or frame-type-specific metrics; if not available, Teleoperation must infer from aggregate metrics and apply heuristics.

Risk: Teleoperation module may become large if it also owns other teleoperation concerns (e.g., latency compensation, operator UI state). Mitigation: Ensure clear separation within Teleoperation between command/telemetry transport (loss mitigation) and higher-level teleoperation logic (e.g., control algorithms, UI synchronization).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target under agreed network conditions). The IM boundary (Teleoperation module) owns the mechanisms (selective retransmission, FEC, network monitoring, adaptive adjustment) needed to achieve this criterion. Verification can be conducted via network simulation, field testing under controlled loss conditions, and continuous monitoring of the Network Link interface metrics. The module's interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert, Telemetry, Network Link) provide clear observability points for validation.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. The FR is clearly allocated to the Teleoperation IM. The DP's four key mechanisms (selective retransmission, configurable FEC, network monitoring, adaptive adjustment) are all contained within Teleoperation's scope. Customer-defined loss thresholds are accepted as configuration inputs via the Network Link or a dedicated configuration interface. The allocation respects separation of concerns: Teleoperation owns transport-layer resilience; Safety Services owns safety logic and consumes network health status; Vehicle owns vehicle-side actuation and control.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Selective retransmission logic for safety signals may require tight coupling with Safety Services' safety thresholds. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface to allow Safety Services to mark frames as safety-critical; Teleoperation applies retransmission policy based on frame criticality flag, not by parsing safety semantics.

Risk: Customer-defined loss thresholds may be scattered across multiple configuration sources (NGHE Solution, Safety Services, Teleoperation). Mitigation: Establish a single configuration service or NGHE Solution-level parameter store; Teleoperation queries thresholds at initialization and subscribes to updates. Avoid embedding thresholds in Teleoperation code.

Risk: Network condition monitoring may duplicate metrics collection if Vehicle or other modules also monitor network state. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface is the single source of truth for network metrics; all modules consume from this interface rather than implementing independent monitoring.

Risk: Adaptive parameter adjustment logic (e.g., FEC strength, retransmission timeout) may become complex and difficult to test if tightly coupled to real-time network fluctuations. Mitigation: Encapsulate adjustment algorithms in a dedicated sub-module within Teleoperation (e.g., AdaptiveController); define clear input/output contracts for network metrics and parameter outputs.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural owner of hybrid loss mitigation. All four DP mechanisms are localized within this module: selective retransmission for critical control frames (Tasking and SafetyAlert inputs), configurable FEC for telemetry streams (Telemetry output), network condition monitoring (Network Link input), and adaptive parameter adjustment (internal logic). The module's interfaces are clean and unidirectional where possible (Telemetry is outbound; Tasking and Network Link are inbound; SafetyAlert is bidirectional for safety signal input and degradation notification output). Minor diffusion exists only in the SafetyAlert interface, which is a necessary and well-defined notification channel, not deep coupling. No evidence of scattered responsibilities or algorithm duplication across module boundaries.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly and completely addresses the FR. FR requires maintaining teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits under agreed network conditions. DP6 specifies a hybrid loss mitigation strategy with four integrated mechanisms: (1) selective retransmission for critical control frames (operator commands, safety signals) ensures command delivery; (2) configurable FEC for telemetry/status streams reduces packet loss impact on telemetry; (3) continuous network condition monitoring provides real-time visibility into loss rates and other metrics; (4) adaptive parameter adjustment based on customer-defined thresholds enables dynamic response to changing network conditions. The DP is implementable, testable against the acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1%), and respects customer-defined thresholds and agreed network conditions. No logical gaps or contradictions between FR and DP.

recommended_revisions: Clarify the Network Link interface specification to ensure it provides stream-specific or frame-type-specific loss metrics (not just aggregate loss rate). If granular metrics are unavailable, document the heuristics Teleoperation will use to infer per-stream loss from aggregate data.

Define the SafetyAlert interface contract explicitly: specify which safety signals require critical-frame treatment, how Safety Services marks frames as safety-critical, and how Teleoperation reports network-induced safety degradation back to Safety Services. This prevents semantic coupling.

Establish a configuration management strategy for customer-defined loss thresholds: specify whether thresholds are static (set at initialization) or dynamic (updated at runtime), and identify the source of truth (NGHE Solution, configuration service, or Teleoperation's own configuration interface).

Document the adaptive parameter adjustment algorithm (e.g., how FEC strength or retransmission timeout is adjusted in response to network metrics). Include acceptance criteria for adaptation responsiveness (e.g., time to detect loss increase and adjust parameters).

Add a sub-module boundary within Teleoperation to separate transport-layer resilience (loss mitigation) from higher-level teleoperation concerns (e.g., latency compensation, operator UI state synchronization) to prevent future scope creep and maintain module cohesion.

Specify the interaction between Teleoperation's adaptive adjustment and Safety Services' safety thresholds: clarify whether Safety Services can request heightened retransmission aggressiveness for safety-critical scenarios, and how this request is communicated (e.g., via SafetyAlert interface or a dedicated override mechanism).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence. The FR is measurable, the DP is logically sound and implementable, and the IM (Teleoperation module) is the correct owner with excellent containment (0.92). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that several critical sub-concerns require explicit decomposition to prevent architectural drift and ensure safety-critical correctness: (1) adaptive parameter adjustment logic is flagged as complex and difficult to test if tightly coupled to real-time network fluctuations—this warrants a dedicated sub-module with clear contracts; (2) SafetyAlert interface coupling with Safety Services' safety thresholds requires explicit specification to prevent semantic coupling; (3) configuration management for customer-defined thresholds needs a clear strategy to avoid scattered sources of truth; (4) network condition monitoring must be explicitly designed to avoid duplication across modules. Given the high safety criticality of this teleoperation system, decomposing these concerns into explicit sub-FRs will improve verifiability, testability, and maintainability. Proceeding to next-level decomposition is justified and necessary.
next_level_sub_frs: FR6.1: Maintain selective retransmission of critical control frames (operator commands, safety-critical signals) to ensure delivery within acceptable latency bounds under agreed network conditions.
FR6.2: Apply configurable forward error correction (FEC) to telemetry and status streams to reduce packet loss impact on operator situational awareness.
FR6.3: Monitor network conditions in real-time (packet loss rate, latency, jitter) and expose metrics via the Network Link interface as the single source of truth for all modules.

FR6.4: Adapt transport-layer parameters (FEC strength, retransmission timeout, selective retransmission aggressiveness) dynamically based on observed network conditions and customer-defined loss thresholds, with responsiveness and stability guarantees.

FR6.5: Coordinate with Safety Services to identify and prioritize safety-critical frames for enhanced retransmission treatment, and report network-induced safety degradation (e.g., loss rate exceeding safety thresholds) back to Safety Services without semantic coupling.

revise.why: The current DP6 is logically correct but lacks sufficient detail to guide implementation and prevent the identified diffusion risks. Specifically: (1) the adaptive parameter adjustment mechanism needs explicit sub-DP definition with clear input/output contracts and stability criteria; (2) the SafetyAlert interface interaction needs explicit specification to prevent tight coupling with Safety Services' safety semantics; (3) the configuration management strategy for customer-defined thresholds must be clarified (static vs. dynamic, single source of truth); (4) the network condition monitoring mechanism must be explicitly designed to ensure it is the single source of truth and does not duplicate metrics collection. Revising the DP to decompose these concerns into explicit sub-DPs will improve clarity and reduce implementation risk.

assumptions: Network Link interface can provide stream-specific or frame-type-specific loss metrics (or heuristics to infer them from aggregate data).
Safety Services can mark frames as safety-critical via the SafetyAlert interface without requiring Teleoperation to parse safety semantics.

Customer-defined loss thresholds are provided via a single configuration source (NGHE Solution, configuration service, or Teleoperation's own interface) and are either static (set at initialization) or updated via a subscription mechanism.
Adaptive parameter adjustment algorithms (e.g., FEC strength, retransmission timeout) can be encapsulated in a dedicated sub-module with clear input/output contracts.
Network condition monitoring is owned by the Network Link interface and is not duplicated by Vehicle or other modules.
Teleoperation's adaptive adjustment can be coordinated with Safety Services' safety thresholds via the SafetyAlert interface or a dedicated override mechanism without introducing semantic coupling.
open_risks: Risk: Adaptive parameter adjustment logic may become complex and difficult to test if tightly coupled to real-time network fluctuations. Mitigation: Encapsulate in a dedicated sub-module (AdaptiveController) with clear input/output contracts and acceptance criteria for adaptation responsiveness.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface may introduce semantic coupling if Teleoperation must parse safety-critical frame types. Mitigation: Define explicit SafetyAlert contract specifying frame criticality flags and safety degradation notifications.

Risk: Customer-defined loss thresholds may be scattered across multiple configuration sources. Mitigation: Establish single configuration service or NGHE Solution-level parameter store; Teleoperation queries at initialization and subscribes to updates.

Risk: Network condition monitoring may duplicate metrics collection if Vehicle or other modules also monitor network state. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface is single source of truth; all modules consume from this interface.

Risk: Interaction between Teleoperation's adaptive adjustment and Safety Services' safety thresholds may be unclear, leading to conflicting parameter adjustments. Mitigation: Define explicit coordination mechanism (e.g., SafetyAlert override, priority rules) in sub-DP for FR6.5.
evidence_needed: Detailed specification of Network Link interface: confirm it provides stream-specific or frame-type-specific loss metrics, or document heuristics for inferring per-stream loss from aggregate data.
SafetyAlert interface contract: specify which safety signals require critical-frame treatment, how Safety Services marks frames, and how Teleoperation reports network-induced safety degradation.
Configuration management strategy: specify whether customer-defined loss thresholds are static or dynamic, identify single source of truth, and document initialization and update mechanisms.

Adaptive parameter adjustment algorithm: document the algorithm (e.g., control law for FEC strength or retransmission timeout adjustment), acceptance criteria for responsiveness (e.g., time to detect loss increase and adjust), and stability guarantees.
Sub-module boundary design for AdaptiveController: define clear input/output contracts, separation from higher-level teleoperation concerns, and testability approach.

Coordination mechanism between Teleoperation and Safety Services: specify how Safety Services can request heightened retransmission aggressiveness for safety-critical scenarios, and how this request is communicated and prioritized.
